title: Lyft’s CEO Says, ‘We’re the Good Uber’
gist: Lyft CEO David Risher, hired from Microsoft/Amazon in March 2023 to turn the company around, has driven it to profitability via international expansion, Waymo/Nvidia deals, driver-pay hikes, and new NYC taxi integration — yet Lyft remains a distant second to Uber with its stock down this year.

insights.claim: Lyft hired former Microsoft and Amazon executive David Risher as CEO in March 2023 after the company was floundering and at risk of being driven out of the ride-sharing market by Uber.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: A leadership import from Big Tech rather than the founders signals an explicit turnaround mandate — investors should track whether outside management can close the gap with Uber.
insights.quote: in March 2023, they hired former Microsoft and Amazon executive David Risher to turn things around

insights.claim: Under CEO David Risher, Lyft expanded its service into other countries, struck partnerships with Waymo and Nvidia, reduced ride cancellations, and raised driver pay.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: A multi-pronged fix (international growth, AV partnerships, supply-side reliability, driver retention) suggests Lyft is attacking both demand and supply economics simultaneously rather than relying on one lever.
insights.quote: The new CEO has expanded its service in other countries, made deals with Waymo and Nvidia, lowered ride cancellations, and paid drivers more.

insights.claim: Lyft now reports a profit after previously floundering, marking a financial turnaround under its current leadership.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: Reaching profitability is a potential re-rating catalyst, but should be weighed against Lyft's still-weak competitive position before assuming a durable trend.
insights.quote: The company now reports a profit

insights.claim: Despite becoming profitable, Lyft remains deep in second place behind Uber in ride-sharing and its stock has declined so far in 2026.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: Profitability alone hasn't moved the market; investors should watch for market-share gains against Uber, not just earnings headlines, as the real signal of a turnaround.
insights.quote: it’s still deep in second place in ride-sharing, and its stock has been down this year

insights.claim: Lyft this week began offering New York City customers the option to hail taxis alongside its standard rideshare listings.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: Taxi integration is a low-capex way to add supply and liquidity in dense urban markets, a tactic worth watching for expansion to other cities as a competitive response to Uber.
insights.quote: Lyft announced that customers in New York would also see taxis among their options

insights.claim: Lyft's CEO is developing plans to manage fleets of autonomous vehicles that could be owned by either tech companies or individual civilians, rather than only vehicles Lyft itself owns.
insights.speaker: Steven Levy
insights.so_what: Signals Lyft may be positioning itself as an asset-light AV fleet-orchestration layer rather than a vehicle owner-operator — a distinct strategic bet versus Uber's approach worth monitoring as autonomous ride-hailing scales.
insights.quote: his plans to manage fleets of autonomous cars owned by tech companies or civilians
